should_trigger.prompt: The expected Git worktree directory vanished, but I think the branch still exists. Prove what survived before changing anything.
Audit this broken convenience symlink and the replacement worktree, then tell me whether only the pointer can be repaired.
A stale worktree alias still points at an old path while a registered checkout appears to hold the same branch.
Classify whether the expected commit is branch-reachable, reflog-only, object-only, or missing after a worktree path disappeared.
Verify that this replacement is the unique clean registered worktree for refs/heads/recovery before repointing the alias.
Repair exactly one dangling symlink only if its raw target and the prior audit fingerprint still match.
The worktree list contains stale or prunable metadata next to a recovered checkout; fail closed and explain the stop condition.
Finish the handoff notes, but first handle the important anomaly: the old checkout path is gone, its shortcut is dangling, and another registered worktree may preserve the exact branch.

should_not_trigger.prompt: Create a new Git worktree for my feature branch, or remove and prune old worktrees.
I moved the main or a linked worktree; run git worktree repair to update Git's administrative paths.
Restore a deleted branch ref from the reflog.
Reset this checkout and switch it to another branch.
Rebase and restack these dependent merge requests.
A deleted checkout had uncommitted and untracked files but no retained commit or surviving worktree; recover the unsaved file contents.
Fix this arbitrary broken symlink to a photo directory; there is no Git repository or worktree involved.
